findings.title: SQL injection via f-string table name interpolation
findings.code_snippet: await db.execute(text(f"DELETE FROM {table} WHERE agent_id = :aid"), {"aid": agent_id})
findings.description: The table name is interpolated via f-string into a raw SQL text() call. While the table names currently come from a hardcoded list (cleanup_tables), this pattern is inherently unsafe. If the list is ever sourced from user input, or if a developer adds a dynamically-constructed table name, this becomes a direct SQL injection vector. The CLAUDE.md security rules explicitly forbid SQL string concatenation. The agent_id parameter is properly parameterized, but the table name is not.
findings.suggestion: Replace the f-string interpolation with SQLAlchemy ORM delete statements using model classes. If raw SQL is unavoidable, use a whitelist validation: assert table in ALLOWED_TABLES before interpolation. Better yet, map table names to SQLAlchemy Table objects and use table.delete().where(table.c.agent_id == agent_id).

findings.title: Pending approvals count not scoped by tenant in stats endpoint
findings.description: The pending_approvals count in get_enterprise_stats is not filtered by tenant_id, unlike the other three metrics (total_agents, running_agents, total_users). This means an admin of tenant A sees the pending approval count from ALL tenants, leaking cross-tenant data. This violates the multi-tenancy invariant documented in CLAUDE.md: 'Every entity is company/tenant-scoped. All queries must filter by tenant_id.'
findings.suggestion: Add tenant scoping by joining through agents: select(func.count(ApprovalRequest.id)).where(ApprovalRequest.status == 'pending', ApprovalRequest.agent_id.in_(select(Agent.id).where(Agent.tenant_id == tid))). This matches the pattern already used in the list_approvals endpoint at line 225-226.

findings.title: Collaboration service lists agents across all tenants
findings.description: list_collaborators returns ALL agents across all tenants that are running or stopped, not just those belonging to the same tenant as the requesting agent. This is a tenant isolation breach. The docstring says 'Returns agents from the same enterprise (same creator's org)' but the implementation does not enforce this. An agent in tenant A can see and collaborate with agents from tenant B.
findings.suggestion: Add tenant_id filtering: select(Agent).where(Agent.id != agent_id, Agent.tenant_id == agent.tenant_id, Agent.status.in_(['running', 'stopped'])). The agent variable is already fetched from DB on line 81, so agent.tenant_id is available.

findings.title: update_permissions accepts untyped dict body with no validation
findings.description: The update_agent_permissions endpoint accepts a raw dict as the request body instead of a typed Pydantic model. This bypasses all input validation: scope_type is not validated against allowed values (could be any string), scope_ids are parsed with uuid.UUID(sid) which raises unhandled ValueError on invalid input, and access_level has only a loose check. The resolve_agent_approval endpoint at line 712 has the same issue. CLAUDE.md requires 'Validate all external input -- syntactic (format) + semantic (business rules). Reject early.'
findings.suggestion: Define Pydantic models: class PermissionUpdate(BaseModel): scope_type: Literal['company', 'user'] = 'company'; scope_ids: list[uuid.UUID] = []; access_level: Literal['use', 'manage'] = 'use'. Similarly define a model for the approval resolution endpoint.

findings.title: Mutable default arguments in SQLAlchemy model columns
findings.description: The Agent model uses mutable dict/list literals as default values for JSON columns (autonomy_policy at line 58, default_skills at line 155, default_autonomy_policy at line 156). In Python, mutable default arguments are shared across all instances. While SQLAlchemy handles this for column defaults at the DB level, the Python-side default= with a mutable dict is a known footgun: modifying one instance's dict before flush can mutate the default for future instances. The correct pattern is default_factory or server_default.
findings.suggestion: Use default=lambda: {"read_files": "L1", ...} (callable default) or use default_factory via dataclass patterns. For simple cases, use server_default=text("'{}'::jsonb") to push the default to the database.

findings.title: enterprise.py router exceeds 685 lines with 7+ responsibilities
findings.code_snippet:
# LLM Model Pool, Enterprise Info, Approvals, Audit Logs,
# Dashboard Stats, Tenant Quotas, System Settings,
# Org Structure, Invitation Codes
findings.description: enterprise.py is a single router file handling 9 distinct domains: LLM Model Pool, Enterprise Info, Approvals, Audit Logs, Dashboard Stats, Tenant Quotas, System Settings, Org Structure, and Invitation Codes. At 685 lines it exceeds the project structure guideline of 400 lines (consider split) and approaches the 800-line mandatory split threshold. This SRP violation makes it hard to maintain, test, and review.
findings.suggestion: Split into focused router modules: api/llm_models.py, api/approvals.py, api/audit_logs.py, api/tenant_quotas.py, api/system_settings.py, api/invitation_codes.py. Each module gets its own router and Pydantic schemas. Register them all in main.py.

findings.title: print() statements used instead of structured logger in main.py
findings.description: main.py uses 22 print() statements for startup logging instead of the structured logging required by CLAUDE.md. While startup logging is a reasonable use case for print (logger may not be configured yet), most of these occur after the logging system is initialized (post-lifespan setup). The print calls include sensitive information like proxy server addresses and node labels. CLAUDE.md forbids console.log/print in production code and requires structured JSON logging.
findings.suggestion: Replace print() calls with logger.info()/logger.warning() using structured context: logger.info('Database tables ready', extra={'step': 'startup'}). For the ss-local proxy, avoid logging server addresses at INFO level; use DEBUG.

findings.title: AgentExecutionEngine middleware system not wired to primary entry point
findings.description: The AgentExecutionEngine is called from call_llm_via_engine in websocket.py, but the primary chat flow in websocket.py still uses the legacy call_llm function (which is 400+ lines of monolithic code). The engine is only used when explicitly called. The middleware ecosystem (ContextMiddleware, MemoryMiddleware, ToolReliabilityMiddleware, FallbackMiddleware) exists but is not the default execution path. This means the middleware features (context budget, memory decay, tool reliability tracking, fallback) are effectively optional/parallel code, risking divergence from the main path.
findings.suggestion: Migrate the primary WebSocket chat flow to use AgentExecutionEngine as the sole execution path. The legacy call_llm function should be deprecated and eventually removed. Add a feature flag to gate the migration if needed.

findings.title: Shadow variable 't' in list_agents template listing
findings.description: The loop variable 't' in the list_templates endpoint shadows the import 't' commonly used for i18n translation in this codebase. While not a bug in Python (no i18n import in this file), it creates confusion when reading the code and could cause issues if i18n is ever added to this module.
findings.suggestion: Rename the loop variable to 'tmpl' or 'template' for clarity: 'for tmpl in templates'.

positive_observations: Path traversal protection in agent_tools.py: file operations resolve paths and check is_relative_to / startswith against the workspace root, preventing directory traversal attacks
Proper parameterized queries throughout: agent_id parameters in SQL text() calls use :aid binding, not string interpolation
Consistent audit logging: all state-changing operations emit audit events with actor, tenant, resource context
Agent lifecycle state machine: transitions are validated through a formal state machine (domain/agent_lifecycle.py) preventing invalid state changes
Secrets encryption: LLM API keys are encrypted at rest via SecretsProvider before being stored in the database
